summary:
The Battle of Sedan, fought on 1–2 September 1870 during the [[event:2535|Franco-Prussian War]], was the decisive engagement that destroyed the main French army, captured Emperor Napoleon III, and sealed the military outcome of the war. It was one of the most stunning examples of the encirclement and annihilation of an entire army in nineteenth-century warfare, and its consequences directly led to the fall of the Second French Empire and the proclamation of the German Empire.

Following the French defeat at the Battle of Gravelotte-Saint-Privat in August, the Army of Châlons under Marshal MacMahon attempted to march north to relieve the besieged Bazaine at Metz. Prussian and German forces under the overall direction of Helmuth von Moltke the Elder tracked the French march and moved to encircle it before it could reach Metz. MacMahon was wounded, and his replacement Wimpffen attempted a breakout that was repulsed. By the morning of 2 September, roughly 100,000 French troops and the Emperor Napoleon III himself were surrounded in the town of Sedan and its surrounding terrain, under fire from hundreds of German guns on the surrounding heights.

Napoleon III, who had been present with the army despite being gravely ill, surrendered personally to King Wilhelm I of Prussia — a moment of extraordinary symbolic power. The capitulation of approximately 104,000 French soldiers was the largest surrender in Western European warfare since Napoleonic times. News of Sedan reached Paris and triggered the revolution of 4 September 1870, which overthrew the Second Empire and proclaimed the Third Republic. The war continued for months, but Sedan had decided its outcome and made German unification under Prussian leadership inevitable.
